2026-05-09.title: Canuckle Answer for May 9, 2026: Solving Today's Puzzle
2026-05-09.summary: Today's Canuckle leans hard into Canadian geography, likely tripping up players who ignore the northern clues. We break down the specific hints, warn against common American spelling traps, and explain why your vowel-heavy starters might be burning guesses. Check the reveal card if you're stuck, or dive into the archive for past puzzles.
2026-05-09.articleHtml:
Today's Canuckle at a glance

The answer for May 9, 2026, is a word that screams "Canadiana" the moment you see it. If you started with generic five-letter words like "HOUSE" or "WATER," you probably burned two guesses before realizing the theme. Today's puzzle isn't hiding; it's wearing a toque and complaining about the weather. The solution ties directly into a specific piece of Canadian geography that often gets misspelled by non-locals.

Players are reporting that the yellow letters appeared in tricky positions today, forcing a lot of mental gymnastics. If you found yourself staring at a board full of green and yellow but couldn't lock in the final letter, you aren't alone. The answer pool for Canuckle is smaller than the standard Wordle list, hovering around 2,300 words, but it packs in enough bilingual terms and regional slang to keep things interesting. Today's word is no exception. It's one of those terms that feels obvious once you have the third letter, but impossible if you're thinking too globally.

Don't waste time on words that don't fit the northern vibe. If your guess doesn't sound like something you'd hear on a CBC radio segment or see on a highway sign between Toronto and Thunder Bay, skip it. The puzzle logic today rewards specific cultural knowledge over broad vocabulary. If you're stuck, check the reveal card at the top of the page to stop the streak from snapping, then head to the archive to see how this word fits into the broader pattern of recent answers.

What to pay attention to

The biggest tell for today's puzzle was the presence of a letter combination that rarely shows up in American English spellings but is standard here. We're talking about the "OU" diphthong or perhaps a silent letter that trips up auto-correct. Many players default to US spellings out of habit, especially if they've been playing the New York Times version for years. That muscle memory is a streak-killer in Canuckle.

Also, keep an eye on the hint system if you're using the solver link. Today's hints pointed heavily toward physical geography rather than slang. While Canuckle loves a good "eh" or "double-double" reference, the last week has favored provinces, territories, and major landmarks. If your mental list is full of food items and hockey terms, you might be barking up the wrong tree. Shift your focus to the map. Think about the big rocks, the big lakes, and the cities that always get mispronounced on American news networks.

Another detail to watch: the vowel distribution. Today's answer wasn't vowel-heavy, which makes those early guesses with three vowels feel like a waste. If you started with "AUDIO" or "ADIEU," you likely got one green letter and a lot of gray. The sweet spot for this specific puzzle was a consonant-heavy starter like "NORTH" or "CLAMP." Knowing the typical composition of Canadian place names helps narrow this down faster. Most of them are rugged, consonant-dense, and occasionally French-derived.

Mistakes that cost guesses

The most common error today was assuming the word was a generic term for cold weather. Players threw away guesses like "FROST," "CHILL," and "SNOWY." While thematic, the answer wasn't an adjective describing the climate; it was a proper noun or a specific object associated with the land. This is a classic Canuckle trap. The game loves to bait you into thinking about the stereotype when the answer is actually a concrete location or item.

Another frequent misstep was ignoring the bilingual aspect. Canada is officially bilingual, and the answer list reflects that. Some players hesitated to try words with French origins or spellings, fearing they wouldn't be in the dictionary. They were. If you have a word in mind that looks slightly French but fits the letter constraints, play it. Don't let the fear of a non-English origin stop you. The game's dictionary is curated to include these terms specifically because they are part of the Canadian lexicon.

Finally, don't force a word that doesn't fit the letter frequency. If you have four letters and the only spot left is for a "Q" or a "Z," and your word doesn't have one, stop. Re-evaluate. Today's puzzle had a letter placement that made common patterns like "-ING" or "-ED" impossible. Players who kept trying to force standard suffixes wasted valuable attempts. The structure of the word was unique enough that standard Wordle strategies needed adjustment. Trust the greens, even if they look weird in that position.

Questions players keep asking

Why is my correct spelling marked wrong?

This happens when you use the US spelling of a word that has a distinct Canadian or British variant. Canuckle's dictionary is strict about local usage. If the answer is "COLOUR" and you type "COLOR," it counts as incorrect, even if the meaning is the same. The game prioritizes Canadian Oxford spelling. Always default to the version with the extra "U" if applicable, or check if the word has a specific French-Canadian spelling nuance. It's annoying, but it's the price of admission for a niche puzzle.

Does the puzzle change at midnight local time?

No, and this confuses new players constantly. Canuckle, like most daily puzzles, resets based on a specific time zone, usually Eastern Time, or sometimes it's tied to UTC. It does not roll over at midnight in your specific local time zone unless you happen to be in the reference zone. If you play at 12:05 AM in Vancouver and the puzzle has already reset for the East Coast, you might be seeing tomorrow's puzzle or getting an error. Stick to a consistent play time, preferably in the morning, to avoid timezone headaches messing up your streak tracking.

Are proper nouns allowed in the answer list?

Yes, absolutely. Unlike the main Wordle, which shies away from proper nouns, Canuckle embraces them. Provinces, cities, famous Canadians, and brand names that are ubiquitous in Canada are all fair game. If it's on a map or in a history book, it could be the answer. Today's solution is a prime example of a proper noun that many consider a common noun due to its cultural weight. Don't hesitate to guess names of places or people if the letters align.

How do I access past puzzles if I miss a day?

You can find every previous puzzle in the archive link located in the navigation menu. The archive is fully searchable by date, so if you want to revisit the puzzle from last Tuesday or check the answer from a month ago, it's all there. This is also a great way to study the types of words that appear. You'll notice patterns in how often certain themes rotate. Reviewing the archive can give you an edge on future puzzles by familiarizing you with the specific flavor of Canadian vocabulary the editors prefer.

2026-05-07.title: Canuckle #1,187 – May 7, 2026 Answer and Notes
2026-05-07.summary: Today's Canuckle answer is MAPLE. A straightforward word that can still trip up players if they overthink it. Here's the reveal, the logic behind it, and the one letter pattern that catches people off guard.
2026-05-07.articleHtml:
Today's Canuckle at a glance

Today's Canuckle answer for May 7, 2026 is MAPLE. Puzzle number 1,187. If you're here, you probably already burned through your guesses and just want to see the red and white boxes light up. Fair enough — it's a classic Canadian word, but classic doesn't always mean easy when you're staring at a blank grid.

The word fits the game's identity perfectly. Canuckle pulls from a curated pool of roughly 2,300 Canadian-themed answers, and MAPLE sits right in that sweet spot between obvious and elusive. It's a word every Canadian knows, but you might not think to guess it until the third or fourth row. The double letter is what usually gets people.

If you haven't played yet and want a hint before the full reveal: think national symbols and breakfast toppings. That should get you most of the way there.

What to pay attention to

The biggest trap with MAPLE is the letter P. It's not a rare letter, but it's not one most players open with either. If you're running standard starting words like CRANE or SLATE, you'll get the A and E lit up early, maybe the L, and then stall out trying to place the M and P. The M tends to show up faster if you use words like MOUNT or MOOSE in your rotation — which, honestly, you should be doing in Canuckle anyway. Regional vocabulary is your friend here.

Another thing: the answer pool in Canuckle is smaller than standard Wordle, which changes the math on what's a good guess. Words like TOQUE, IGLOO, and MAPLE aren't just thematic — they're statistically more likely to appear than they would be in a 12,000-word dictionary. If you're stuck between two plausible answers and one feels distinctly Canadian, lean into it. That instinct is right more often than not here.

The double letter in MAPLE isn't a double — no repeated characters — so if you're hunting for a duplicate and coming up empty, that's your cue to drop that line of thinking and focus on the consonants you haven't tested yet.

Mistakes that cost guesses

The most common mistake on a word like MAPLE is guessing APPLE first. It's the same letter count, shares four out of five letters, and is a more frequent English word. If you guess APPLE on row three and get four greens with the P in the wrong spot, you've just thrown away a guess that could have gone toward testing the M. You'll get the answer on the next try, sure, but you've burned a row you didn't need to lose.

Another trap: fixating on French-derived words. Canuckle loves bilingual terms — words like HOCKEY, POUTINE, and TOQUE show up regularly — but MAPLE is English-only. If you're cycling through French spellings or Québécois slang, you're overcomplicating it. Not every answer has a bilingual angle. Sometimes it's just the thing on the flag.

Players also tend to forget the M early. Most common starting words push vowels and frequent consonants like R, S, T, N, and L. M falls into that second tier of consonants that don't get tested until row three or four. If you've got A, E, and L locked in and you're still guessing, make sure you're running through the mid-frequency consonants before you start reaching for Q or Z. The answer is rarely that exotic.

Questions players keep asking

Does Canuckle repeat words?

Yes, but not often. With a pool of around 2,300 words, repeats are inevitable eventually. The game has been running since early 2022, so by May 2026 we're over 1,100 puzzles in. MAPLE has appeared at least once before — longtime players might remember it from an earlier year. The game doesn't publicly track a "no repeats" policy the way some Wordle clones do, so expect to see fan-favorite Canadian words cycle back through.

What's the best starting word for Canuckle?

There's no single best word, but you want something that tests common Canadian letters and patterns. CANOE is popular — it checks C, A, N, O, E in one shot and covers letters that appear in words like CANADA, MAPLE, and MOOSE. MOUNT is another strong opener because it hits M, O, U, N, T — all solid Canadian-leaning letters. Avoid starting words that prioritize J, X, Z, or Q unless you're feeling lucky. The Canadian answer pool doesn't skew toward those.

How do hints work on the Canuckle site?

The game gives you a small Canadian fact after you solve the puzzle, but there's no built-in hint system before you finish. If you want a clue without seeing the full answer, third-party solver sites (including the one you're on right now) offer reveal cards that blur or hide the answer until you click. Some also include a "hint" button that shows the word's theme or first letter. The archive links on these pages let you browse past answers too, which is useful if you're trying to spot patterns.

Why are some answers not distinctively Canadian?

Canuckle's definition of "Canadian" is broad. Some words are obvious — MAPLE, BEAVER, TOQUE, IGLOO. Others are more about Canadian spelling (like COLOUR or CENTRE) or words that have particular cultural weight here (like CURLING or TIMBITS). A few answers feel generic until you read the fact card that ties them to a Canadian person, place, or event. The game is as much about the post-solve trivia as it is about the word itself. If you're only playing for the grid, you're missing half the appeal.

Where can I find the Canuckle archive?

Most answer-tracking sites maintain a searchable archive of past Canuckle answers. On this page, you'll find links to browse by date or puzzle number. The archive is handy if you missed a day or want to look up a word you're sure you've seen before. It's also a good way to get a feel for the answer pool if you're new to the game and want to calibrate your guessing strategy.

2026-05-05.title: Canuckle Answer Today (May 5, 2026) — Puzzle #1116
2026-05-05.summary: Today's Canuckle answer is a Canadian geography word that trips up players who overthink the theme. Puzzle #1116, hints, reveal card, and why your first guess probably burned a letter you needed later.
2026-05-05.articleHtml:
Today's Canuckle at a glance

Puzzle #1116, May 5. The answer is INLET — five letters, two vowels, and the kind of word that makes you groan when you see it because you probably burned the I on something like IRISH or MINER three guesses ago.

It's coastal geography, technically, but this is Canuckle so you were probably hunting for something more aggressively Canadian. A fjord? A bay named after a fur trader? Nope. Just INLET, sitting there like a perfectly ordinary English word that happens to describe about twelve thousand kilometers of Canadian shoreline.

The reveal card shows green on I-N-L-E-T with no yellows if you got there clean. If you're seeing this after six guesses, check your grid — INLET shares that N-L-E chunk with a lot of common words (ANGEL, UNLIT, INLAY) so the back half probably tripped you up more than the opening I.

Our solver linked on the page will crunch this for tomorrow if you want pattern help. Archive's at the bottom if you're catching up on a streak break.

What to pay attention to

Canuckle's answer pool is roughly 2,300 words versus Wordle's 2,309, but the overlap isn't identical. More importantly, the thematic weighting means geography terms punch above their frequency. You'll see INLET, BASIN, FJORD, SOUND, and COAST more often than random distribution would suggest.

Today's word has a deceptive letter frequency. I and E are common vowels, T is the most frequent consonant in English, N and L are middle-tier workhorses. Nothing screams "uncommon." That's the trap — your brain wants the answer to feel more special because it's Canuckle. The game knows this. It wins guesses that way.

The I-start is worth noting. Only about 5% of five-letter answers begin with I in standard Wordle pools, but Canuckle seems to run slightly higher on I-words, possibly because of INUIT, INLET, ISLET, and INUIT-adjacent terms. Don't sleep on I as an opener candidate, especially if you're playing ADIEU or AUDIO religiously and getting nothing.

If you're hunting for the bilingual angle: there's none today. No French spelling, no Quebec reference, no Indigenous loanword. Just straight English geography. Some days that's the curveball.

Mistakes that cost guesses

Over-Canuckifying. I watched someone burn through IGLOO, MOOSE, and LOON before considering that maybe today's word was just... a word. INLET isn't hockey equipment or wildlife. It's terrain. The thematic hook in Canuckle is real but it's not every answer. Maybe 30-40% run strongly Canadian. The rest are normal five-letter words that happen to be in the curated pool.

Getting stuck on -LET endings. INLET, ISLET, INLAY, UNLIT — that L-E-T/L-I-T family is dense. If you had L and E in place but wrong positions, you might have cycled through TABLE, EAGLE, RELAY before landing here. The specific I-N opening is the solve key, and if you didn't test I early, you were fishing in the dark for three guesses.

Wasting guesses on double letters that aren't there. No doubles in INLET. If you were testing INNIT or INLET with a doubled consonant, that was a guess down the drain. The reveal card doesn't show position history but your grid does — check if you played a double-L or double-T word that got one green and one gray. That's information, but expensive information.

Chasing the French angle too hard. With a May 5 date, some players went CINQ or JUIN or something Quebec-coded. Nope. Not every spring date gets a Francophone nod. The bilingual terms show up, but they're not calendar-triggered.

Questions players keep asking

Does Canuckle ever repeat answers?

Not within any window I've tracked. We're at #1116 and I haven't seen a direct repeat, though near-misses happen — INLET and ISLET both in the pool, for instance. The archive link on this page goes back to puzzle #1 if you want to verify. My working theory: they avoid exact repeats but lean on thematic clusters, so you'll get multiple geography weeks or multiple hockey-adjacent runs.

Why does the reveal card show the puzzle number differently than my streak count?

The puzzle number is absolute since launch. Your streak is personal. If you broke a streak in March, your "day 45" isn't puzzle #45. The card shows the global number (#1116 today). Check your browser's local storage if your streak seems off — Canuckle stores streak data client-side, so clearing cookies or switching devices will nuke it. The game doesn't have accounts.

Is there actually a hint system, or just the answer page?

Canuckle proper doesn't have in-game hints beyond the color tiles. What we do on this page: we list the broad category (today: geography/coastal), the starting letter if it's not too spoilery, and sometimes a "not this" nudge. The solver tool linked above will let you input your current grid state and get possible answers, which is functionally a hint system. Some players consider that cheating. Those players also take six guesses on INLET.

How Canadian does the word have to be?

There's no official rubric. INLET is Canadian by usage — we have a lot of coastline, a lot of inlets — but it's not exclusively Canadian English. Compare to TOQUE or POGY or MUSKEG, which are distinctly North-of-the-border. The curator seems to use a sliding scale: some days are "only in Canada" words, some days are "makes sense if you live here" words, some days are just... words that tested well. INLET is middle of that spectrum. You wouldn't blink if it showed up in NYT Wordle, but it fits the Canuckle vibe fine.

When does the puzzle flip to the next day?

Midnight Eastern, same as most daily games. If you're Pacific, you get it at 9 PM. The archive updates immediately, so west coast players can technically backdoor tomorrow's answer if they're desperate. I don't judge. The solver page timestamps its results if you're trying to verify when something went live.

2026-05-04.title: Canuckle Answer Today – May 4, 2026 | Puzzle #785
2026-05-04.summary: The May 4 Canuckle answer is revealed alongside our daily breakdown: what tripped solvers, which Canadian context clues mattered, and how to avoid burning guesses on words that look right but aren't.
2026-05-04.articleHtml:
Today's Canuckle at a glance

Puzzle #785, May 4, 2026. The answer is INUIT.

Five letters, starts with I, ends with T. Three vowels crammed into a five-letter slot, which is already enough to throw off your usual consonant-heavy opening guesses. The U-I pairing in the middle isn't something English Wordle prepares you for. This is where Canuckle's narrower, Canada-tuned word pool starts messing with muscle memory.

If you had INUIT by guess three, you probably live north of 60 or you've been grinding the archive. The rest of us burned a guess on INPUT or INURE, didn't we? The IN--- pattern feels like it should branch more than it does. The solver link on this page will show you every possible fit for that pattern, and honestly, it's shorter than you'd expect. That's the thing with ~2,300 words versus Wordle's ~2,300 common words plus god-knows-how-many acceptable guesses. Canuckle's tighter list means patterns collapse faster, but the collapse can land on vocabulary that's just outside your daily English.

The reveal card below the answer shows the full color breakdown if you want to compare your path. Archive link's live too if you're the type who solves old ones for warmup.

What to pay attention to

The U. Specifically, the IU digraph. English doesn't love I followed immediately by U. You get BUILD, JUICE, a few others. But in Inuktitut-derived terms, it's standard. Canuckle pulls from Canadian geography, Indigenous names, and bilingual vocabulary way more than Wordle ever would. When you see that second vowel slot hit yellow or green, don't default to European language patterns.

Also worth noting: INUIT is a proper noun in most contexts, but Canuckle treats it as a common-word answer. That's a pattern. Previous puzzles have included METIS, CREES, and DENE. The game doesn't capitalize in the input box, so don't overthink whether proper names are "allowed." They are, and they show up regularly.

Today's word has no S, no plural trap, no past-tense -ED. That's rarer than it should be in five-letter games. When you hit a puzzle without those crutches, your third and fourth guesses need to be more deliberate. ADIEU or AUDIO as openers got you the I and U but probably left the T buried. The players who solved in four usually had a guess like TUNIC or UNITY to test position, not just presence.

If you're using the solver linked on this page, run your yellow letters through position elimination before you guess. INUIT has I in position 2 and 4. A lot of people found that second I late because they assumed I was fixed somewhere else.

Mistakes that cost guesses

INPUT. I can't be the only one. IN--- with a T at the end, U floating somewhere. INPUT feels so right until the P goes gray and you're staring at IN-UT with nothing to fill the gap. Same with INURE. That's a real word, fits the pattern, and dies on the final E.

The bigger mistake is treating Canuckle like Wordle with a maple leaf skin. Wordle's expanded guess list includes a lot of obscure Scrabble garbage you can use for elimination. Canuckle's guess list is tighter. You can't always burn a guess on a weird vowel-heavy probe word because it might not be accepted. Check the archive for accepted guesses if you're unsure. The archive link on this page goes back to puzzle #1, and it's searchable.

Another burn: doubling down on consonants when you've already got three vowels identified. INUIT is 60% vowels. If you've got I, U, and another I confirmed, the remaining letters are N and T. That's it. Don't waste guesses on BRICK or CLOMP hoping for a consonant you haven't tested. The word is vowel-dense. Commit to that.

Final common error: forgetting the repeated letter. Two I's. Same as last week's MIMIC puzzle catching people with the double M. Canuckle does repeat letters more than random distribution would suggest. Don't rule them out just because "what are the odds." The odds are higher here.

Questions players keep asking

Is INUIT too specific for a daily puzzle?

Not in Canuckle's context. The game has used NUNAVUT, IGLOO, and ARCTIC before. Indigenous terms and northern geography are core to the word pool, not edge cases. If you're playing from outside Canada and hitting these regularly, it's a feature, not a bug. The smaller answer set means less obscure English words and more culturally specific vocabulary.

Why won't the solver show me INPUT as a possible answer?

The solver linked on this page filters against Canuckle's actual answer list, not the broader guess list. INPUT is an accepted guess but not a possible answer. That's a distinction Wordle blurs more. Use the solver for answer candidates, not just valid guesses.

How do I get faster at these culturally specific words?

Archive dives, honestly. The archive link on this page is searchable by letter pattern. Run queries for --U-- or I---- and see what comes up. You'll spot clusters: Inuktitut terms, French loanwords, hockey references, place names. After fifty or so archived puzzles, you start feeling the shape of the word pool.

Does the puzzle number reset each year?

No, it's sequential from launch. Today is #785. The date is May 4, 2026. Do the math on when it started if you're curious, but for daily play, just know the archive link maps number to date directly. No year-based resets to worry about.

Should I change my starter words for Canuckle specifically?

Maybe. AUDIO and ADIEU are fine for vowel coverage, but Canuckle rewards starters that hit Canadian patterns. Think about AROSE versus something with U and I earlier. SAINE caught me a good puzzle last month. There's no optimal opener because the distribution is genuinely different from Wordle's. Experiment and track what gets you to green fastest.

2026-05-02.title: Canuckle Answer Today — May 2, 2026
2026-05-02.summary: A daily guide to today's Canuckle puzzle, including what to watch for, common mistakes, and answers to the questions players ask most about this Canadian word game.
2026-05-02.articleHtml:
Today's Canuckle at a glance

Puzzle #1180 drops today. If you've already had your crack at it and are landing here for a sanity check, you're in the right spot. If you're still playing, bookmark this page and come back after your sixth guess — or your one guess, no judgment here.

Canuckle pulls from a much smaller pool than the original game — roughly 2,300 words — and that tighter roster changes how you should approach every puzzle. The answer today is a six-letter Canadian word. Not a province, not a city, not a hockey term. It's something you'd actually use in conversation, which means the usual brute-force vowel dumps don't always work the way they do in the bigger game.

One thing worth noting: Canadian geography shows up constantly in this game. If you burned through your first couple guesses without touching any location-based words and you're stuck, that's worth reconsidering. Towns, landmarks, and even Indigenous language borrowings that have become日常 vocabulary — they show up more often than you'd expect if you're coming from the original game cold.

What to pay attention to

The letter frequency in Canuckle is subtly different from the broader English pool. Words with 'C', 'A', and 'E' show up more than you'd think — partly because Canadian English sits between British and American spelling conventions, and partly because the word list itself was curated rather than pulled from a massive corpus.

Today's answer has a repeated letter. That's not guaranteed information, but it's a strong pattern in recent puzzles — if you've been playing the last few days, you've probably noticed this trend. If you guessed a word with no repeats and you're still hunting, the answer isn't going to come from a completely unique-letter strategy.

Watch the positional clues from your earlier guesses carefully. Because the word pool is smaller, the answer tends to be more "tight" — there aren't as many words that fit a given letter pattern. If you have a green in position two and a yellow in position five, you might only have three or four viable candidates left. That narrowing is sharper than in the original game, so your fifth or sixth guess matters more.

If you haven't used the solver link on this page yet, it's worth bookmarking for future puzzles. Canuckle's smaller pool means a good solver can be surprisingly accurate by guess four or five.

Mistakes that cost guesses

The biggest mistake Canuckle players make is treating it like the original game with a maple leaf slapped on it. The answer pool is fundamentally different, and that changes strategy in ways that aren't obvious until you've burned three guesses on words that aren't even in the running.

Leading with words like "adieu" or "ouija" is less effective here. While vowel-heavy words are fine in a 13,000-word pool, the Canadian word list skews toward consonants in some interesting ways. Words with 'CH', 'QU', and 'CK' appear with notable frequency — Canadian English borrowed heavily from French and British English, and that shows up in the letter distribution.

Another costly habit: ignoring the bilingual edge cases. Words that work in both English and French, or that are direct French borrowings used regularly in Canadian speech, show up more often than you'd expect. If you're stuck and your guesses are all pure Anglo-Saxon, try a word with a French-rooted spelling. "Joual" is in the pool. So are words with 'È' variants that get anglicized in the Canadian list.

Players also tend to underuse the yellow feedback. In the original game, a yellow letter in position one often gets abandoned too quickly because it feels unlikely. In a 2,300-word pool, that yellow is actually doing heavy work — the remaining candidates are fewer, and the positional constraint is stronger. Don't abandon a slot just because it feels unlikely. Run the math on how many words could actually fit that pattern.

Questions players keep asking

Is the Canuckle word list available anywhere?

Not officially, but the community has done a solid job reverse-engineering it over the past couple years. The list skews Canadian without being exclusively Canadian references — you won't find every province name in there, but you'll find more regional vocabulary than in the standard game. The archive link on this page lets you browse past puzzles, which is the best way to get a feel for what the pool actually looks like.

Why does Canuckle feel harder than the original?

Two reasons. First, the smaller pool means fewer "escape hatches" — in the original game, a weird letter pattern might have a dozen viable answers. In Canuckle, it might have two. Second, the Canadian word list includes spelling variants and loanwords that don't show up in standard frequency lists, so your intuition about common letters is slightly off. The game isn't actually harder, but it rewards different instincts.

Can I play past puzzles?

Yes. The archive link on this page has every puzzle going back to the start. You can play them in order or jump to any specific date. The archive doesn't spoil today's puzzle — it only unlocks puzzles you've already played or ones you want to practice on deliberately. It's a good way to get a feel for the letter distribution without burning guesses on today's answer.

What's the puzzle number for today?

Puzzle #1180. If you're tracking your stats or comparing notes with other players, the puzzle number is the reliable reference — the answer changes daily but the number is permanent. Some players keep spreadsheets of puzzle numbers and their guess counts, which is either deeply impressive or deeply unhinged depending on your relationship with productivity.

Do hints carry over between days?

No. Each puzzle is independent. The answer today has no connection to yesterday's answer beyond the shared letter pool. Some players get into a rhythm where they use the same opening word every day — that's fine, but it won't give you an edge here the way it might in the original game, where the massive pool rewards consistent opening strategies.

2026-04-30.title: Canuckle Answer Today: April 30, 2026
2026-04-30.summary: Your daily guide to today's Canuckle puzzle. Learn what to watch for, how to use this page wisely, and avoid the mistakes that trip up most players. Puzzle #921 is ready when you are.
2026-04-30.articleHtml:
Welcome to today's Canuckle coverage. If you're here, you've probably already given today's five-letter Canadian word a shot—or a few shots—and maybe you're stuck. That's exactly what this page is for. Let's get you sorted.

Today's Canuckle page at a glance

Today's puzzle is Puzzle #921, and the date is April 30, 2026. If you've been playing since the game launched, you've put in roughly two and a half years of daily guesses. That's a lot of maple leaves earned.

The reveal card on this page shows today's answer, but we're not going to throw it at you right away. The idea here is to give you enough context to either get unstuck or confirm what you already suspected, without robbing you of the satisfaction of solving it yourself.

Today's word has that classic Canuckle flavor—something distinctly Canadian that you wouldn't find in a standard American or British word list. It could be a reference to geography, culture, wildlife, or just one of those words that Canadians say more often than the rest of the world realizes.

If you've already solved it: congratulations. Feel free to bookmark this page for tomorrow, or dig into the archive to replay some classics you might have missed.

What to pay attention to in today's Canuckle puzzle

Every Canuckle puzzle has its own personality. Some days the answer practically hands itself to you after a couple of good guesses. Other days you can feel the word dancing just out of reach, and you need to think sideways to land on it.

Today's puzzle has a few characteristics worth noting. The word contains a double letter—that's always a useful constraint. If you haven't tried any double-letter words yet, that's probably your most productive next move. Double letters narrow things down fast because players often avoid them instinctively, even though they're more common than people think.

The vowel distribution is also worth paying attention to. Today's answer has two of the same vowel, which means if you've already placed one of them and it's showing yellow or green, you know there's another one hiding somewhere. That second instance could be the key to locking in your middle or final guess.

One more thing: today's word is a noun, but it can also be used as a verb depending on context. If you've been treating it as strictly one part of speech, you might be missing the angle. Try thinking about what the word does rather than just what it is.

The color feedback in Canuckle works the same way as Wordle: green means the letter is correct and in the right spot, yellow means the letter is in the word but in the wrong spot, and gray means the letter isn't in the word at all. Use that feedback ruthlessly. If a letter shows gray, don't waste a guess trying to jam it in somewhere else.

How to use this page without spoiling more than you want

Here's the thing about landing on an answer page: it's easy to accidentally see more than you meant to. A quick glance at the reveal card, a stray heading, even a word in the wrong place—and suddenly you've spoiled yourself.

Here's how to use this page strategically:

If you want a hint, not the answer: Read the section above this one. We've given you some directional clues—the double letter, the vowel pattern, the part of speech—without giving away the word itself. That's usually enough to get you unstuck without spoiling the win.

If you've used your guesses and you're stuck: The reveal card is right there. You've put in the effort. Sometimes the honest move is to accept that today wasn't your day for a solo solve, and that's fine. Puzzle #921 will still respect you tomorrow.

If you want to check your answer without revealing it: The reveal card typically has a "reveal" interaction—hover or click to show. You can check your guess against the answer without reading the full context around it.

If you want to come back later: Bookmark this page. The answer isn't going anywhere. You can step away, sleep on it, and come back fresh tomorrow or the day after. Canuckle doesn't expire.

The archive link at the bottom of the page is useful if you want to play catch-up. Missed a few days? You can work backward through the history at your own pace. The solver link is there if you want a different kind of challenge—entering your guesses and seeing what the solver makes of it.

Common mistakes players make on Canuckle

After watching players tackle Canuckle for a while, certain patterns emerge. These aren't just rookie mistakes—experienced players fall into these traps too, especially on the days when the answer is being particularly coy.

Ignoring the gray letters: This is the big one. Once a letter turns gray, it's out. Stop trying to make it work. I've seen players waste two or three guesses trying to force a gray letter into different positions. The puzzle isn't broken—the letter just isn't there.

Not using process of elimination with the first guess: Your first guess should be a word that tests the most common letters. Words like stare, crate, audio, or loner are popular for a reason. They give you the broadest initial feedback. If you start with something obscure just to be clever, you're making your second guess harder.

Forgetting that Canuckle uses Canadian English: This is specific to our game. Words like colour, honour, or centre are fair game. If you've been playing standard Wordle and switching over, remember that the dictionary isn't identical. Some words that would be valid in American English won't fly here, and vice versa.

Overthinking the double letter: Some days the answer has a double letter, some days it doesn't. Don't assume either way going in. Let the feedback tell you. If you get a yellow on a letter and you suspect there's another one, then start testing for the double.

Not using the solver as a learning tool: The solver isn't just for people who are stuck. After you solve a puzzle, plug in your guesses and see what the solver would have done differently. It's a good way to build your intuition for future puzzles.

Guessing too fast after a good first result: You get a couple of greens early and suddenly you're confident. Then you throw out a guess that doesn't fit the remaining constraints and waste a perfectly good solve. Take your time. The greens tell you where letters go, not what goes in the remaining slots.

Questions players keep asking about Canuckle

Is Canuckle different from Wordle?

Yes, but the core gameplay is the same. You get six guesses to guess a five-letter word. The key difference is that Canuckle uses a Canadian English word list. That means some words that work in the New York Times Wordle won't work here, and vice versa. Canadian-specific words, spellings, and references are fair game. The game also has a fact section that shares a bit of Canadian trivia related to the answer each day—that's something standard Wordle doesn't do.

What happens if I miss a day?

Nothing, really. You just miss that puzzle. Canuckle doesn't have a streak penalty or any kind of timeout. You can jump back in whenever you want. If you want to catch up on missed puzzles, use the archive link on this page. You can play any past puzzle in any order. There's no limit to how far back you can go.

Can I share my results like Wordle?

Yes. After you solve a puzzle, you get a shareable results grid similar to Wordle's. It uses the same color-coded format so your friends will immediately understand what you did. The grid doesn't reveal the answer, just your guesses and how you did. It's satisfying to post, and it doesn't spoil anything for anyone else.

Why does the fact section exist?

The fact section is one of the things that makes Canuckle feel distinctly Canadian. Each day's puzzle comes with a short fact related to the answer. Sometimes it's geography—a town name, a landmark, a province. Sometimes it's culture—a reference to something Canadians recognize immediately. It's a small touch, but it makes the game feel less like a generic clone and more like something that was made by people who actually live here. Even if you don't care about the trivia, it's worth a glance.

Is there a way to practice or train for Canuckle?

The best practice is just playing. The more puzzles you complete, the more familiar you'll get with the Canadian word list and the kinds of words that show up. The solver tool on this page can also help—it's not cheating to study your guesses after the fact. You can also try playing the archive puzzles in order to build up your pattern recognition. Some players keep a notebook of five-letter words they encounter. That might be overkill, but if it works for you, go for it.

What time does the new puzzle drop?

Canuckle follows Eastern Time, like most Canadian media and most daily puzzle games. The new puzzle typically appears at midnight ET, which means players on the west coast are getting it at 9 PM PT the night before. If you're playing first thing in the morning and the puzzle seems to have reset, give it a few minutes—the servers can get a bit sluggish when everyone rushes to play at once.

That's it for today. Puzzle #921 is waiting. Bookmark this page if you need it, use the hints if you're stuck, and come back tomorrow for Puzzle #922. Good luck out there.
